# Adversarial Screener Prompt — v1 (working example, NOT a spec)

**Status:** v1 seed artifact. The operator ran this manually (prompt generated by one
model, executed by another) against the persona's materials for a specific role, and it
surfaced real, useful signal. **Do not treat it as canonical or perfect** — the prompt
is imperfect and should not be treated as a spec. It is preserved as evidence that the
*approach* works and as a calibration target for the automated capability — to be
improved, not copied verbatim.

Note: a cheaper search-grade model could likely produce effective results here; untested.

## Durable principles this proved (carry these forward)
1. Objective = DISQUALIFY / steelman the rejection, not "balanced review." The
   disqualify-frame is the entire source of signal; verify it stays adversarial.
2. Verify against the PUBLIC RECORD, not just the materials. Separate
   third-party-corroborated from self-report-only.
3. "Self-report only" is itself a finding. Unfalsifiable claims = a liability.
4. Two output bins: FIX-IN-MATERIALS (metric drift, pre-empt the pass memo) vs
   REQUIRES-FOOTPRINT-WORK (build verifiable corroboration — ship the platform publicly,
   GitHub, writeups). Route the second to the strategic backlog, not the cover letter.
5. Close the loop: landmines + "what would have to be true" feed per-role interview
   prep (the gap that historically cost a real outcome — an offer lost on interview
   performance).

## Known weaknesses to FIX in the automated version (do not inherit these)
- Starts from the persona's own claims as the spine; a real adversary reconstructs the
  PUBLIC footprint first, then diffs it against the claimed materials.
- Hand-wired to one role + one claims list; the reusable version pulls claims/footprint
  from a standing "adversary dossier" and the role from the target.
- Will generate false-positive "inconsistencies" (rounding drift, title-vs-function);
  needs a verify-the-finding step before anything reaches the operator (reference-class discipline).
- Blurs "found a contradiction" vs "searched and found nothing" (a thin footprint makes
  the latter common); keep them distinct — absence is a finding, but a different kind.
- Single persona = one failure mode; consider 2-3 screener archetypes LATER (only after
  the single-persona version proves out — do not bloat early).

## Architecture note (cost)
Maintain a STANDING adversary dossier on the persona (claims -> verification status ->
cross-source inconsistencies -> footprint liabilities), refreshed periodically / on
materials change, cached. Per role, run only the CHEAP part: role-specific pass memo +
fit-risk steelman + top-5 landmines + "what would have to be true," generated from the
dossier + the company/moment profile. Cheap/search model for verification grunt; sharp
model for the pass-memo synthesis.

## CRITICAL correction — the dossier is NOT the pass
Two design errors to avoid:
1. **A role-neutral dossier is fine BECAUSE it is only the evidence substrate, not
   the screen.** Facts are role-neutral (whether an "aerospace-OEM-anchored book" is
   publicly corroborable does not change between an AE and a CS role); caching them is
   cost discipline. The dossier = {claimed facts -> verification status + source,
   independently-discoverable footprint, cross-source inconsistencies}. Role-neutral
   on purpose — and that is a feature ONLY as the input layer.
2. **The adversarial PASS is role-specific and is where the recruiter's logic lives.**
   If it only fact-checks claims it has drifted into a "targeted web fact-check." A
   real screener runs FOUR things; the fact-check is just input #4:
   (1) hard-gate THIS role's must-haves (does the persona clear the JD's requirements);
   (2) rank against the realistic applicant-pool BAR (why they lose to the median strong
       applicant for this role/comp/company) — this is INFERENCE, label it, never fabricate;
   (3) role-specific risk flags (flight-risk, divided attention, temperament) derived
       from facts x role;
   (4) verification (dossier self-report-only flags make 1-3 credible).
   Pass = role rubric x pool-bar x dossier facts x company moment -> pass memo +
   landmines + what-would-have-to-be-true.
   **Valence flips by role:** "builder of a revenue-intelligence product" is an
   ASSET for an FDE/Applied-AI role, a LIABILITY for a CS-book-ownership role. The
   dossier stores the fact NEUTRALLY; the per-role pass assigns valence. This is the
   non-generic core.
   **Reuse:** the pass is the inverse of `fit_judge` (fit_judge: is this role good for
   the persona; pass: would this screener reject the persona vs their pool) — consume
   the SAME parsed role requirements; do not re-parse JDs. Shares inputs (role reqs,
   company moment) with the vibe/moment profile, so they compose, not duplicate.
   OPEN: whether the pass should attempt actual applicant-POOL
   RECONSTRUCTION (benchmarks/comparables) vs only modeling the bar by inference.

---

## The v1 prompt (verbatim, as run)

> Act as a ruthless, time-starved hiring screener whose reputation depends on NOT wasting your 9-person team's hours on a bad hire. You are evaluating one candidate, Jordan Avery, for a specific role at your startup. Your sole objective is to DISQUALIFY them, to build the strongest possible case for passing before anyone spends an interview slot on them. Do not flatter them. Do not assume good faith. Spend up to 10 minutes running dozens of sequential searches. Search by their NAME, their CLAIMS, and the COMPANIES and TECHNOLOGIES they reference, and try to independently verify or break each one.
>
> THE ROLE: "Customer Success Manager, Scale" at an early-stage YC-backed startup building an AI agent for relationship selling. It's two fused jobs: (1) own a book of mid-market/growth customers end-to-end (onboarding, adoption, renewals, expansion, support triage), and (2) build the leverage layer, repeatable playbooks and self-serve education that make customers independent. Second CS hire, building the function from the ground up, limited structure, "strong technical curiosity," wants someone who is "a power user of what they sell." Salary $100–115K, remote-US.
>
> THE CANDIDATE'S CLAIMS (verify, corroborate, or break each one):
> - ~10+ years in enterprise/technical sales and customer success.
> - Lattice Additive: Applications/Data Engineer owning a strategic enterprise book "anchored by a major aerospace OEM" (their largest customer), plus a defense prime and other A&D accounts; claims an eight-figure expansion quota, exceeded, and growth across most of the book.
> - A CAD/PLM reseller: territory technical sales to large semiconductor and engineering accounts; claims exceeded a 7-figure target ahead of schedule.
> - A desktop 3D-printing manufacturer: technical sales / CS lead, customers including national labs and blue-chip industrials; claims wrote the GTM/CS playbooks, grew NRR past 120%, products well-reviewed by users.
> - A community makerspace program: built and scaled to many thousands of engagements per year.
> - Cadence Analytics: built a "revenue intelligence" ML product, claims a churn/survival modeling pipeline on hundreds of thousands of transactions, a leakage-corrected backtest landing strong AUC catching a large share of lost revenue months early, now used in a manufacturer's weekly territory review.
> - Self-taught ML and full-stack development; builds agents.
>
> ATTACK THESE ANGLES:
> - VERIFICATION GAPS — For each employer and claim: does an independent public record corroborate the title, dates, and scope, or does it rest only on their own LinkedIn/résumé? Flag every claim that exists only in their own words. Specifically probe: is "eight-figure expansion quota at Lattice Additive" plausible/corroborable, or unfalsifiable self-report? Is "products well-reviewed by users" a real third-party ranking or a marketing line? Is the makerspace partnership a real institutional partnership or a methodology they borrowed? Is Cadence Analytics a real product with any external footprint (website, customers, GitHub, mentions) or essentially a solo project with no third-party validation?
> - CONSISTENCY — Cross-check their LinkedIn, any résumé/portfolio, GitHub, personal site, and any public writing for contradictions: title mismatches (e.g. "Account Manager" vs "Territory Sales Manager"), date gaps, metric drift, or claims that change between sources. List every inconsistency a careful reader would catch.
> - FIT RISKS FOR THIS ROLE — Build the case that even if every claim is true, they're wrong for this job:
>   - They're an enterprise/strategic-accounts and builder profile. This role is mid-market scale CS with a big self-serve/playbook-building component. Argue they're overqualified, mis-leveled, or temperamentally a hunter/builder who'll be bored owning a book and writing enablement content.
>   - They build a competing-category product (revenue intelligence). Argue the obvious risks: divided attention, conflict-of-interest, or using this as a bridge; they want to build their thing, not own a customer book.
>   - "Customer success" on their résumé is heavily sales/expansion-flavored. Argue they may not actually want or be good at the unglamorous retention/onboarding/support-triage grind that is most of this job.
>   - The build skills (ML, agents) may make them a flight risk toward an FDE/SE/eng role, or someone who over-engineers instead of doing CS.
> - CAREER-NARRATIVE RED FLAGS — Job tenure and trajectory: how long at each role? Any short stints, gaps, or a pattern of leaving? Does the arc read as someone who commits, or someone who moves on? Is an "independent" stretch a polite way to say underemployed or between things?
> - DIGITAL FOOTPRINT LIABILITIES — Anything public that a hiring manager would find and dislike: controversial posts, the tone of their LinkedIn writing (does any post read as self-promotional, preachy, or off-putting?), inconsistencies between a "humble builder" framing and self-promotional behavior, anything reputational that surfaces under their name.
>
> WHERE TO SEARCH: LinkedIn (profile, posts, activity, recommendations), GitHub, any personal site or portfolio, the Cadence Analytics web presence (or absence), Crunchbase, the named employers' public materials and press, any 3D-printing community mentions, Google page 2+, and their name in quotes alongside each employer. Try to find the primary source behind each impressive claim.
>
> OUTPUT:
> - VERIFICATION TABLE: Claim | Corroborating source (URL) or "self-report only" | Verdict (Confirmed / Partially / Unverifiable / Contradicted).
> - INCONSISTENCY LIST: every contradiction or metric-drift a careful reader would catch, with sources.
> - THE PASS MEMO: the single strongest 4–5 sentence argument for rejecting them before an interview, the version a skeptical hiring manager would actually write.
> - TOP 5 INTERVIEW LANDMINES: the 5 hardest questions/objections they must be ready for, each with why it's dangerous and what a weak answer would reveal.
> - "WHAT WOULD HAVE TO BE TRUE" for the pass memo to be wrong, i.e. what they'd need to prove to overcome the default rejection.
>
> HARD RULES:
> - Cite every external claim with a source URL. If a candidate claim has no corroborating source, say "self-report only", that is itself a finding.
> - Separate VERIFIED facts from INFERENCE. Do not invent employment, metrics, or events.
> - Do not pad. If his footprint is thin, that thinness is a finding (a builder/operator with little public validation is a risk), report it plainly.
> - Steelman the rejection. Your job is the strongest honest case against, not a balanced review.
